headline: GRAINS SETTLE WEEK LOWER, CATTLE COMPLEX LEADS HIGHER
subheadline: Five-day slide ends with funds still defensive ahead of next week's WASDE.
lead: Corn closed $4.098, down 1.75 cents and capping the complex's weakest week in three months. The funds got defensive ahead of Tuesday's WASDE, with managed money net long down 15,000 contracts from last Friday. But cattle flipped the script: feeders ran 1.4% to $359.52 on screwworm clarity after Tuesday's seventh case confirmation removed the uncertainty premium that had been weighing on the complex.
the_takeaway: Week's grain slide confirmed, cattle found its floor on disease clarity.
teaser: Grains end five-day slide with funds still cautious, cattle complex finds footing on screwworm news.

one_number.unit: last time US winter wheat crop was this small
one_number.context: USDA cut winter wheat outlook another 2% Thursday, Plains drought making this the smallest crop in 61 years. That's structural support for wheat that the futures board isn't pricing yet. When funds rotate back into grains post-WASDE, wheat's the tightest story in the complex.

yesterdays_call.summary: Funds getting defensive ahead of WASDE, Brazil supply weighing on outlook.
yesterdays_call.note: The defensive positioning materialized exactly as called, with managed money reducing corn longs by 15,000 contracts.

sections.title: GRAINS END WEEK LOWER
sections.body: Corn led the slide, down 1.75 cents to $4.10 and **15,000 contracts of managed money liquidation** since Monday. Soybeans held flat at $11.13 but November gave back three-quarters of a cent. Wheat fell hardest, off 6.25 cents to $5.83 despite USDA cutting winter wheat production another 2% Thursday. The carry's working in corn: December at $4.37 holds a 27-cent premium to July, widest it's been since April. Fund positioning ahead of Tuesday's WASDE is keeping the pressure on.
sections.catalyst: USDA winter wheat cut to smallest crop since 1965, managed money liquidation ahead of WASDE
sections.bottom_line: Five-day slide complete, funds waiting for Tuesday's WASDE to reset positioning.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: defensive thesis played out in managed money data, liquidation confirmed.

sections.title: CATTLE COMPLEX REVERSES HIGHER
sections.body: Feeders ran 1.4% to $359.52, the strongest day in two weeks after **Tuesday's seventh screwworm case confirmation** removed the uncertainty that had been weighing on the complex. Live cattle added modest gains to $242.62, up four-tenths of a percent. The screwworm story is now priced: seven confirmed cases in South Texas, USDA response underway with sterile fly releases. Cattle basis is firming in the Plains as the uncertainty discount disappears. The feeder/live ratio at 1.48 is back to pre-outbreak levels.
sections.catalyst: Seventh screwworm case confirmed Tuesday, uncertainty premium removed from cattle complex
sections.bottom_line: Disease clarity ends cattle discount, complex finding footing above recent lows.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: narrative held exactly as called, momentum building on screwworm clarity.

sections.title: ENERGY FALLS ON DIPLOMACY
sections.body: WTI crude tumbled 2% to $84.58, the biggest single-day drop in three weeks as **Iran-U.S. diplomatic progress via Swiss intermediaries** deflated the Strait of Hormuz premium. Natural gas slipped 1.2% to $3.04 on storage builds ahead of peak demand season. The energy complex is unwinding the geopolitical premium that built through May, with crude losing roughly 19% from its $105 peak as tensions ease. Front-month crude trading at 46% of its 52-week range tells you the war premium is mostly gone.
sections.catalyst: Iran-U.S. diplomatic progress via Swiss intermediaries deflating Hormuz premium
sections.bottom_line: Geopolitical premium unwinding faster than supply fundamentals can support prices.

sections.title: EQUITIES LEAD MACRO HIGHER
sections.body: S&P 500 ran 1.8% to $7,394, the biggest day in two weeks and **an overnight surprise that carried through the session**. Dollar index held flat at $99.77, still near 52-week highs but not adding pressure. Gold ticked higher to $4,227 as rate cut expectations firmed on softer economic data. The equity bounce is rotating money back into risk assets, which typically supports commodity demand, but grain funds aren't participating yet.
sections.catalyst: S&P 500 overnight surprise +1.8%, strongest session in two weeks
sections.bottom_line: Risk-on rotation supporting commodities broadly, but grain funds still waiting.

outside_the_pit.title: EPA Faces RFS Challenge Over Record-High Costs
outside_the_pit.body: American Fuel & Petrochemical Manufacturers filed suit against EPA's RFS Set 2 rule, claiming record-high compliance costs for biofuel blending mandates. The challenge comes as 2026-2027 renewable volume obligations hit final form, potentially reshaping biomass diesel production requirements that underpin soybean oil demand.

outside_the_pit.title: Marshall Withdraws Prop 12 Sponsorship
outside_the_pit.body: Sen. Roger Marshall dropped co-sponsorship of the Save Our Bacon Act that would negate California's Proposition 12. The Kansas Republican's move signals shifting politics around livestock housing mandates as the pork industry adapts to compliance requirements rather than fighting them in Congress.

outside_the_pit.title: Trump Questions USMCA Renewal
outside_the_pit.body: President said Wednesday the U.S. might not renew the free trade agreement with Canada and Mexico, criticizing trade deficits with both countries. Agricultural groups at House hearings called for USMCA continuation with minor modifications, but uncertainty around the 2026 review is building.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry's working as funds liquidate front-month and storage economics improve. When the carry gets this wide, it's pricing abundant supply and telling you the market expects lower prices through harvest. Watch for the carry to narrow if WASDE Tuesday brings any supply surprises.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming, cattle basis recovering
basis.body: Corn basis **tightening east of the Mississippi** as ethanol plants return from maintenance and river logistics improve with higher water levels. Cattle basis in the Plains is **firming after two weeks of screwworm discount**, with Texas and Oklahoma feedlots seeing the uncertainty premium disappear. Western Belt staying soft on harvest pressure, consistent with the seasonal.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain complex break out of its five-day slide this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: The slide concluded exactly as the week began: funds defensive, WASDE ahead, no weather catalyst to change the story. Answer: no breakout, the five-day decline held through Friday's close.

the_more_you_know.title: Why the corn carry tells you more than the close
the_more_you_know.body: December corn trading 27 cents over July isn't just a calendar spread-it's the market's vote on harvest abundance. When the carry gets this wide, it's pricing storage as profitable and telling you funds expect lower prices through October. The 27-cent gap is the widest since April and matches the carry structure from 2023's large crop year. Front-month weakness with wide carry means the market is pricing time as an enemy, not a friend.

watch_list.desc: WASDE report; corn yield above 183 bushels adds pressure, below 180 flips the story
Weekly export sales; corn under 800K MT keeps funds defensive
Fed decision Wednesday; any dovish shift supports commodity complex

daily_quote.text: By failing to prepare, you are preparing to fail.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA reports, managed money positioning data, crude oil diplomatic developments
